data.description:
## Why

The audit UI needs an optional, disabled-by-default, read-only local web surface that is fully inert
when off. Establishing that surface — the packaging extra, the config flag, lazy imports, the
loopback binding, and a running server that renders one real page end-to-end — is the riskiest
architectural piece (gating + packaging + binding), so it ships first as a thin vertical slice that
proves the whole optional-surface mechanism.

## What

A `rebar audit serve` subcommand that, when enabled, starts a FastAPI server (reusing the
`reviewbot` web stack) bound to 127.0.0.1, serving a read-only INDEX page listing tickets that have
audit data (each linking to a per-ticket page that the sibling rendering story fills in). Gated by a
new default-false `[ui] enabled` config flag and a new `nava-rebar[ui]` optional extra; all web
imports are lazy so disabled / absent-extra = inert.

## Endpoint access contract (human/admin auth)

The server binds 127.0.0.1 (loopback) ONLY and serves READ-ONLY content; there is no state-changing
endpoint. Human access control is the loopback boundary — no network principal can reach it — which
is the justified no-auth posture (matching datasette / mlflow / `git instaweb --local`). Binding a
non-loopback host requires an explicit `--host` flag that prints a warning; actually exposing the
service to a network (and any auth that would then be required) is OUT of scope for this epic —
documented, not implemented. No machine credentials are involved.

## Scope

- `pyproject.toml` — add `[project.optional-dependencies] ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]",
  "jinja2"]` (reusing the reviewbot stack), and document the extra.
- `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — add a `UiConfig` with `enabled: bool = False` (+ an `_as_bool`
  coercer) giving env `REBAR_UI_ENABLED`; document the key in `docs/config.md`.
- New `src/rebar/audit/server.py` (kept < 500 LOC) — the FastAPI app + a `serve()` entry, lazy-
  imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on
  `ModuleNotFoundError`; binds 127.0.0.1; one index route using the read layer from story E.
- CLI `rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port <n>]`.

## Approach rationale

Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack rather than adding Flask or a bespoke `http.server`, so the
repo carries one web framework; lazy-import it inside the command (never at package init) so
`import rebar` stays dependency-free — the exact pattern the reviewbot extra already proves. Ship a
thin vertical slice (server + one real index page) rather than plumbing-only, so the risky
packaging/gating/binding path is exercised end-to-end from the first story.

## Compatibility & rollback

Default-off and behind an extra; `import rebar` unchanged (no eager web import). Rollback = flag off
/ extra not installed. No data or schema change.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] With `[ui] enabled = false` (default), `import rebar` imports no web dependency and
      `rebar audit serve` refuses to start with a message naming the flag. Oracle: a test asserting
      no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar` and the disabled path does not start a server.
- [ ] With the `ui` extra absent, `rebar audit serve` exits with an actionable "install
      nava-rebar[ui]" message, not a traceback. Oracle: a unit test simulating `ModuleNotFoundError`
      on the lazy import and asserting the message + non-zero exit.
- [ ] With the feature enabled, the server binds 127.0.0.1 and serves an index page listing tickets
      that have audit data. Oracle: a live end-to-end test starting the server on an ephemeral port
      and asserting the index returns HTTP 200 and lists a seeded audited ticket (test-design §3
      server/CLI).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.
data.title: audit web server scaffolding (subcommand, [ui] extra, config flag, loopback)
